an3.60:1.1: Then the brahmin Saṅgārava approached the *Bhagavā*, and having approached, exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
an3.60:1.2: Having exchanged pleasantries and cordial words, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the brahmin Saṅgārava said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an3.60:1.4: We ourselves perform sacrifices, and we have others perform sacrifices.
an3.60:1.5: In that regard, Master Gotama, both the one who performs the sacrifice and the one who has others perform it — all of them are practicing the path of merit that benefits many bodies, that is, on account of the sacrifice.
an3.60:1.6: But one who, Master Gotama, goes forth from home into homelessness from whatever family, disciplines only one, calms only one, brings only one to final *nibbāna* — such a one is practicing the path of merit that benefits one body, that is, on account of the going forth."
an3.60:10.1: And here, brahmin, someone does not read minds by means of a sign, but having heard the voice of humans, non-humans, or *devatās*, reads minds:
an3.60:10.2: 'Your mind [*citta*] is thus, your mind [*citta*] is like this, your mind [*citta*] is such.'
an3.60:10.3: Even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
an3.60:11.1: And here, brahmin, someone does not read minds by means of a sign, nor having heard the voice of humans, non-humans, or *devatās*; but having heard the sound produced by the spread of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*] in one who is thinking and examining, reads minds:
an3.60:11.2: 'Your mind [*citta*] is thus, your mind [*citta*] is like this, your mind [*citta*] is such.'
an3.60:11.3: Even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
an3.60:12.1: And here, brahmin, someone does not read minds by means of a sign, nor having heard the voice of humans, non-humans, or *devatās*, nor having heard the sound produced by the spread of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*]; but having encompassed with his own mind [*citta*] the mind [*citta*] of one who has attained a *samādhi* without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicara*], he knows:
an3.60:12.2: 'However this person's mental *saṅkhāras* are directed, immediately after this mind [*citta*], he will think the thought [*vitakka*] called such-and-such.'
an3.60:12.3: Even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
an3.60:12.4: This, brahmin, is called the marvel of mind-reading.
an3.60:13.1: And what, brahmin, is the marvel of instruction?
an3.60:13.3: 'Think [*vitakka*] thus, do not think [*vitakka*] thus; attend thus, do not attend thus; abandon this, attain this and dwell in it.'
an3.60:13.4: This, brahmin, is called the marvel of instruction.
an3.60:13.5: These, brahmin, are the three marvels.
an3.60:13.6: Of these three marvels, brahmin, which marvel do you prefer as more excellent and more refined?"
an3.60:14.1: "In that regard, Master Gotama, as for the marvel where someone experiences many kinds of psychic powers …
an3.60:14.2: and wields bodily mastery as far as the brahma world —
an3.60:14.3: this marvel, Master Gotama, is experienced only by the one who performs it; only for the one who performs it does it accrue.
an3.60:14.4: To me, Master Gotama, this marvel appears to be akin to an illusion.
an3.60:15.1: As for the marvel too, Master Gotama, where someone reads minds by means of a sign:
an3.60:15.2: 'Your mind [*citta*] is thus, your mind [*citta*] is like this, your mind [*citta*] is such' — even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
an3.60:15.3: And here, Master Gotama, someone does not read minds by means of a sign, but having heard the voice of humans, non-humans, or *devatās*, reads minds …
an3.60:15.4: nor having heard the voice of humans, non-humans, or *devatās*, but having heard the sound produced by the spread of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], reads minds …
an3.60:15.5: nor having heard the sound produced by the spread of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], but having encompassed with his own mind [*citta*] the mind [*citta*] of one who has attained a *samādhi* without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicara*], he knows:
an3.60:15.6: 'However this person's mental *saṅkhāras* are directed, immediately after this mind [*citta*], he will think the thought [*vitakka*] called such-and-such' — even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
an3.60:15.7: This marvel too, Master Gotama, is experienced only by the one who performs it; only for the one who performs it does it accrue.
an3.60:15.8: To me too, Master Gotama, this marvel appears to be akin to an illusion.
an3.60:16.1: But as for this marvel, Master Gotama, where someone instructs thus:
an3.60:16.2: 'Think [*vitakka*] thus, do not think [*vitakka*] thus; attend thus, do not attend thus; abandon this, attain this and dwell in it' —
an3.60:16.3: this marvel, Master Gotama, I prefer as the most excellent and most refined of these three marvels.
an3.60:17.1: Wonderful, Master Gotama! Marvelous, Master Gotama!
an3.60:17.2: How well this has been said by Master Gotama! And we hold Master Gotama to be endowed with these three marvels.
an3.60:17.3: For Master Gotama experiences many kinds of psychic powers … and wields bodily mastery as far as the brahma world,
an3.60:17.4: for Master Gotama, having encompassed with his own mind [*citta*] the mind [*citta*] of one who has attained a *samādhi* without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicara*], knows:
an3.60:17.5: 'However this person's mental *saṅkhāras* are directed, immediately after this mind [*citta*], he will think the thought [*vitakka*] called such-and-such.'
an3.60:17.7: 'Think [*vitakka*] thus, do not think [*vitakka*] thus; attend thus, do not attend thus; abandon this, attain this and dwell in it.'"
an3.60:18.1: "Indeed, brahmin, those were words spoken presumptuously and boldly to my face;
an3.60:18.3: For I, brahmin, experience many kinds of psychic powers … and wield bodily mastery as far as the brahma world.
an3.60:18.4: For I, brahmin, having encompassed with my own mind [*citta*] the mind [*citta*] of one who has attained a *samādhi* without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicara*], know:
an3.60:18.5: 'However this person's mental *saṅkhāras* are directed, immediately after this mind [*citta*], he will think the thought [*vitakka*] called such-and-such.'
an3.60:18.7: 'Think [*vitakka*] thus, do not think [*vitakka*] thus; attend thus, do not attend thus; abandon this, attain this and dwell in it.'"
an3.60:19.1: "But is there, Master Gotama, even one other *bhikkhu* endowed with these three marvels, apart from Master Gotama?"
an3.60:19.2: "Not just one hundred, brahmin, nor two hundred, nor three hundred, nor four hundred, nor five hundred — in fact there are far more *bhikkhus* who are endowed with these three marvels."
an3.60:19.3: "And where, Master Gotama, do those *bhikkhus* dwell at present?"
an3.60:2.1: "Well then, brahmin, I will ask you about this very matter. Answer as you see fit.
an3.60:2.3: Here a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, perfectly enlightened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, well-gone, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be tamed, teacher of gods and humans, a Buddha, a *Bhagavā*.
an3.60:2.5: 'Come, this is the path, this is the practice; having followed it, I proclaim the unsurpassed culmination of the holy life, realized by direct knowledge for myself;
an3.60:2.6: come, you too practice accordingly, so that you too, having so practiced, will attain and dwell having realized by direct knowledge for yourselves the unsurpassed culmination of the holy life.'
an3.60:2.7: Thus this teacher teaches the *Dhamma*, and others practice for that purpose — and those number many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands.
an3.60:20.2: Just as, Master Gotama, one might set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what had been hidden, or point out the path to one who was lost, or hold up a lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes might see forms — in the same way, the *Dhamma* has been illuminated by Master Gotama in many ways.
an3.60:20.3: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, and to the *Dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus*.
an3.60:20.4: May Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for as long as life lasts."
an3.60:3.2: Given this, is the path of merit that benefits one body or many bodies, on account of the going forth?
an3.60:3.3: "Given this, Master Gotama, the path of merit that benefits many bodies applies, on account of the going forth."
an3.60:4.1: When this was said, Venerable Ānanda said this to the brahmin Saṅgārava:
an3.60:4.2: "Of these two paths of yours, brahmin, which path do you prefer — the one with less effort, less striving, greater fruit, and greater benefit?"
an3.60:4.3: When this was said, the brahmin Saṅgārava said this to Venerable Ānanda:
an3.60:4.4: "Just like Master Gotama and Master Ānanda —
an3.60:4.5: these are worthy of my veneration, these are worthy of my praise."
an3.60:5.1: A second time, Venerable Ānanda said this to the brahmin Saṅgārava:
an3.60:5.2: "I am not asking you this, brahmin:
an3.60:5.3: 'Who is worthy of your veneration, who is worthy of your praise?'
an3.60:5.4: I am asking you this, brahmin:
an3.60:5.5: 'Of these two paths of yours, brahmin, which path do you prefer — the one with less effort, less striving, greater fruit, and greater benefit?'"
an3.60:5.6: A second time, the brahmin Saṅgārava said this to Venerable Ānanda:
an3.60:5.7: "Just like Master Gotama and Master Ānanda —
an3.60:5.8: these are worthy of my veneration, these are worthy of my praise."
an3.60:6.1: A third time, Venerable Ānanda said this to the brahmin Saṅgārava:
an3.60:6.2: "I am not asking you this, brahmin:
an3.60:6.3: 'Who is worthy of your veneration, who is worthy of your praise?'
an3.60:6.4: I am asking you this, brahmin:
an3.60:6.5: 'Of these two paths of yours, brahmin, which path do you prefer — the one with less effort, less striving, greater fruit, and greater benefit?'"
an3.60:6.6: A third time, the brahmin Saṅgārava said this to Venerable Ānanda:
an3.60:6.7: "Just like Master Gotama and Master Ānanda —
an3.60:6.8: these are worthy of my veneration, these are worthy of my praise."
an3.60:7.2: "Even when asked a legitimate question a third time by Ānanda, the brahmin Saṅgārava evades it and does not answer.
an3.60:7.4: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to the brahmin Saṅgārava:
an3.60:7.5: "What, brahmin, was the conversation that arose today among the king's men assembled and gathered in the royal inner chamber?"
an3.60:7.6: "This was the conversation that arose today, Master Gotama, among the king's men assembled and gathered in the royal inner chamber:
an3.60:7.7: 'In the past there were fewer *bhikkhus*, yet more performed the marvel of psychic power that surpasses human capacity;
an3.60:7.8: but now there are more *bhikkhus*, yet fewer perform the marvel of psychic power that surpasses human capacity.'
an3.60:7.9: This was the conversation that arose today, Master Gotama, among the king's men assembled and gathered in the royal inner chamber."
an3.60:8.3: The marvel of psychic power, the marvel of mind-reading, and the marvel of instruction.
an3.60:8.4: And what, brahmin, is the marvel of psychic power?
an3.60:8.5: Here, brahmin, someone experiences many kinds of psychic powers: 'Having been one, he becomes many; having been many, he becomes one; he appears and vanishes; he passes through walls, through enclosures, through mountains, unimpeded, as if through space; he dives in and out of the earth as if it were water; he walks on water without sinking as if it were earth; seated cross-legged, he travels through the sky like a winged bird; with his hand he touches and strokes the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful; he wields bodily mastery as far as the brahma world.'
an3.60:8.6: This, brahmin, is called the marvel of psychic power.
an3.60:9.1: And what, brahmin, is the marvel of mind-reading?
an3.60:9.2: Here, brahmin, someone reads minds by means of a sign:
an3.60:9.3: 'Your mind [*citta*] is thus, your mind [*citta*] is like this, your mind [*citta*] is such.'
an3.60:9.4: Even if he reads much, it turns out just so, not otherwise.
